summary: I was living at 602 South Windbrig Avenue in Springfield, Ohio when I was drafted into the Army. It was from the draft board in Springfield that I was sent to Fort Knox, Kentucky on the same day. Springfield was my home before and after my military service, and it’s where I returned after the war ended in October 1945.

Before joining the Army, I had two years of ROTC training at Wilberforce University. That military training was recognized when I entered the service, and it helped me become one of the captains training tankers at the Armed Forces Replacement Training Center. Wilberforce played a key role in preparing me for my leadership duties in the Army.

I was drafted into the United States Army during World War II and served for almost four years. My role was primarily training tankers and later transporting troops and supplies to the front lines. The Army was both challenging and rewarding, and I earned several medals including one for the Battle of the Bulge.

At the Armed Forces Replacement Training Center, I was chosen as a captain to train tankers, thanks to my ROTC background. This was at Fort Knox, Kentucky, where I spent nearly four years preparing men for tank battalions like the 84th and 64th. The training included physical fitness, weapon handling, and tank operation, which was crucial for the war effort.

I arrived at Fort Knox, Kentucky the same day I was drafted, after leaving Springfield and Columbus. Fort Knox was where I underwent basic training and later served as a trainer for tank crews at the Armed Forces Replacement Training Center. It was also where I experienced racial discrimination, especially regarding access to Louisville, Kentucky nearby.

Part of my training duties involved preparing men for the 84th Tank Battalion. Alongside the 64th, these battalions were the focus of our tanker training at Fort Knox. Although I didn’t see direct combat with these units, training them was an important contribution to the war effort.

Similar to the 84th, I helped train soldiers for the 64th Tank Battalion at the Armed Forces Replacement Training Center. My role was to ensure these tank crews were proficient with their weapons and vehicles before heading to combat zones. This training was vital to prepare them for the challenges of war.

One of my most memorable experiences was during the Battle of the Bulge. Our truck company was trapped by German forces after they broke through the American lines near Normandy. We had to escape under heavy fire after a barrage was laid down, and I even had a bullet hit my rifle. Surviving that was a moment I celebrated with a bottle of German Schnapps.

We operated near Normandy during the war, including taking English soldiers there to relieve American troops. It was near Normandy that we experienced the German breakthrough leading to the Battle of the Bulge. I remember driving trucks out of a dangerous situation there and navigating the roads carefully to avoid enemy fire.

I was stationed in Germany after the war ended, where everyone was celebrating victory. I recall a soldier recklessly firing his carbine in celebration, which was a scary moment for me. Germany was also where I received some of my medals and experienced the relief that the war was finally over.

As a member of the United States Army, I served from my drafting in Springfield, Ohio through training at Fort Knox and deployment overseas. My role was primarily in training tankers and transporting supplies and troops during World War II. The Army was both a place of camaraderie and challenge, especially facing discrimination and combat stress.

After surviving a dangerous encounter during the Battle of the Bulge, I celebrated by drinking a bottle of Schnapps, which is German whiskey. That bottle helped me relax and rest for several days after the intense experience. Schnapps became a memorable symbol of relief and survival during the war.

Fort Knox, Kentucky was where I spent the first days of my service and nearly four years training tankers at the Armed Forces Replacement Training Center. It was also a place where racial discrimination was evident, especially regarding access to Louisville. Despite these challenges, Fort Knox was central to my military experience.

When we landed on Cherbourg Beach, I used my compass skills to help correct our course after the company commander initially went the wrong direction. Cherbourg was an important landing point as we moved into Belgium and further into Europe. My navigation skills there helped keep us safe and on track.

I spotted a sign for Saint-Lô during our movement in Europe, which helped me realize our company commander was heading the wrong way. Saint-Lô was south of our position, and correcting our direction was crucial for reaching Belgium safely. This experience showed how important map and compass reading was in the field.

Our mission took us into Belgium after landing in Normandy and correcting our course near Cherbourg. Belgium was part of the route during the Battle of the Bulge and other operations. Traveling through Belgium was tense because we were in combat zones and had to be constantly alert.

I spent about two weeks stationed outside Paris, which was a wonderful break during my six to eight months overseas. While there, I visited many historical sites like the Louvre, the Tuileries Garden, the Palace of Versailles, the Eiffel Tower, and Notre-Dame Cathedral. Seeing these places was a highlight of my service and something I had only read about before.

During my time near Paris, I visited the Louvre, although all the paintings were gone because of the war. Even without the artwork, it was impressive to see the walls and the building itself. Visiting the Louvre was part of a memorable tour of Parisian landmarks during my service.

I had the chance to visit the Tuileries Garden while stationed near Paris. It was part of the historical sites I toured, including the Louvre and the Palace of Versailles. The garden was a peaceful and beautiful place amidst the chaos of war.

Visiting the Palace of Versailles outside Paris was a highlight during my service. I got to see the castle of Louis XIV and experience a place I had only read about before. It was a rare moment of cultural enrichment during the difficult times of war.

Fontainebleau was one of the historic places I visited while stationed near Paris. It was part of the tour that included many famous landmarks. Seeing Fontainebleau gave me a greater appreciation for French history during my time overseas.

I had the opportunity to tour the Eiffel Tower while stationed near Paris. It was an unforgettable experience to see such an iconic symbol of France up close. The visit was part of a brief but memorable leave during my six to eight months overseas.

I visited the Cathedral of Notre-Dame de Paris after the war, adding to my experiences of French landmarks. It was a remarkable place to see, especially after all the destruction caused by the conflict. Notre-Dame was part of the rich cultural heritage I got to witness during my service.

While stationed at Fort Knox, we faced discrimination that prevented us from going into Louisville comfortably. Eventually, we pushed back by showing up in uniform and demanding access, which led to us receiving passes to enter the city. Louisville was close by but initially off-limits due to segregation policies.

Clark County, Ohio is where Springfield is located, which was my hometown before and after the war. It’s where I lived when I was drafted and where I returned after my service ended. Clark County represents my roots and the community I came back to after the war.

During my service, I didn’t have much contact with family or correspondence because we were always on the move and communication was difficult. The United States Postal Service was not really part of my experience overseas since letters and mail were hard to send or receive. Staying in touch was simply not possible during much of my deployment.
